senses.definition: a sharp expression of one's disapproval or criticism of someone as a result of their action or behavior

examples.x: The manager got a flea in her ear from the client when they expressed dissatisfaction with the product's quality.
The politician received a flea in his ear during the press conference when reporters questioned the inconsistencies in his statements.
When the employee offered a flea in his ear about the inefficiencies in the company's processes, the manager took immediate action to address the issues.
As the project deadline approached, the team members got a flea in their ears about the potential delays and worked diligently to address the issues and meet the target.

senses.definition: to criticize someone severely because of what they said or did

examples.x: After the team's poor performance, the coach had a go at the players for their lack of effort during the game.
During the meeting, the manager had a go at the employee for making repeated mistakes in the project report.
The teacher had a go at the students for not taking the assignment seriously and submitting subpar work.
In the press conference, journalists had a go at the spokesperson for providing vague and evasive answers to critical questions.
The parent had a go at their child for breaking the rules and not following the agreed-upon guidelines.

senses.definition: to angrily warn or threaten someone so that they will not make the same mistake

word_list.pronunciation: ɹˈeɪk ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ ˌoʊvɚ ðə kˈoʊlz

senses.definition: to severely criticize someone because of their mistake

examples.x: Dissatisfied with the team's performance, the manager decided to rake them over the coals in the project review.
The teacher raked the students over the coals for their lack of preparation and participation in class.
During the performance evaluation, the supervisor raked the employee over the coals for consistent underperformance.

word_list.pronunciation: dɹˈæɡ ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ ˌoʊvɚ ðə kˈoʊlz

senses.definition: to subject someone to severe criticism

examples.x: He shrugged off the thought of his superiors dragging him over the coals for making such a statement to a civilian.

senses.definition: to violently criticize or punish a person over what they did or said
to strongly criticize someone or something for something bad that has happened

examples.x: Disappointed with the team's poor performance, the coach decided to whale on the players during the post-game analysis.
Faced with the budget crisis, the board of directors began to whale on the finance department for its oversight in financial planning.
The teacher didn't hesitate to whale on the students who were caught cheating on the exam.
In the crisis meeting, the CEO whaled on the project manager for the delays that jeopardized the company's reputation.

senses.definition: (of criticisms or warnings) failing to have any effect on a person

examples.x: Despite the harsh comments from the critics, the actor remained calm and collected, letting it all be water off a duck's back.
The team captain faced intense scrutiny from the media, but he brushed off the criticism like water off a duck's back.
Her coworkers' gossip and rumors were like water off a duck's back; she focused on her work and ignored the negativity.
The politician learned to let the constant attacks from opponents be water off a duck's back, staying focused on their goals.
No matter how challenging the circumstances, the resilient leader faced adversity with a stoic demeanor, allowing criticism to be like water off a duck's back.
As the spokesperson for the company, she found herself in the firing line during the press conference, addressing tough questions from journalists.
The project manager knew they were in the firing line when the client expressed dissatisfaction with the delayed deliverables.
In the competitive industry, staying innovative is crucial for companies that want to remain on the firing line.
The outspoken politician always puts themselves in the firing line by expressing controversial opinions on social issues.

senses.definition: to experience the effects or consequences of actions, often from others

examples.x: As the project manager, he was on the receiving end of both praise and criticism for the team's performance.
The customer service representative was on the receiving end of numerous complaints about the product defects.
During the negotiation, the company was on the receiving end of the competitor's aggressive pricing strategy.
After making a mistake in the presentation, he was on the receiving end of corrective feedback from the supervisor.

senses.definition: used to refer to the act of doing something with a lot of energy and excitement

examples.x: The fans were in full cry as their team scored the winning goal in the championship game.
During the heated debate, both politicians were in full cry, passionately defending their positions.
As the election campaign gained momentum, the supporters were in full cry, canvassing neighborhoods and rallying voters.
The children were in full cry, playing and laughing in the park on a sunny afternoon.
In the bustling marketplace, vendors were in full cry, promoting their products and calling out to potential customers.

word_list.pronunciation: θɹˈoʊ pˈoːɹ kˈoʊld wˈɔːɾɚɹ ˌɑːn ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to demotivate a person by talking negatively about their plans or opinions

examples.x: When she excitedly shared her innovative business concept, her skeptical colleagues quickly threw cold water on the idea, highlighting potential flaws.
Despite his enthusiasm for the project, his supervisor consistently poured cold water on his proposals, creating a sense of frustration.
Every time he brought up the possibility of pursuing a career in the arts, his pragmatic parents would throw cold water on his dreams, urging him to choose a more stable path.
The team was buzzing with excitement about the new marketing strategy, but the senior executive's critical remarks threw cold water on their optimism, prompting a reconsideration of the approach.

word_list.word: the pot calling the kettle black
word_list.pronunciation: ðə pˈɑːt kˈɔːlɪŋ ðə kˈɛɾəl blˈæk

senses.definition: used when someone is criticizing another for a fault that they have as well

examples.x: Jane accused Mark of being lazy, but it was like the pot calling the kettle black since she was often found taking long breaks during work hours.
The politician criticized his opponent for accepting campaign donations, but it was a case of the pot calling the kettle black, as he had his own financial backers.
Sarah accused her friend of gossiping too much, but it was the pot calling the kettle black because she was known for sharing rumors herself.
The teacher scolded the students for not paying attention in class, but it seemed like the pot calling the kettle black, given that the teacher frequently checked their phone during lectures.
When the manager accused the team of lacking professionalism, it felt like the pot calling the kettle black, considering the manager's own habit of arriving late to meetings.

senses.definition: to face strong negative feedback or verbal abuse

examples.x: After the project failed to meet expectations, the team had to take a beating during the post-mortem meeting, enduring harsh critiques from the stakeholders.
The athlete took a beating in the media for his poor performance in the championship, facing criticism from sports analysts and fans alike.
During the heated debate, the politician had to take a beating from opponents who questioned his integrity and policy decisions.
The author's latest book received mixed reviews, and they had to take a beating from literary critics who found fault with the plot and character development.
In the competitive business world, entrepreneurs often have to take a beating from market forces, adapting to challenges and learning from failures to ultimately succeed.
